title: `podman build` incorrectly issues 'missing build arguments' warnings for multi-stage builds
body:
<!--
If you are reporting a new issue, make sure that we do not have any duplicates
already open. You can ensure this by searching the issue list for this
repository. If there is a duplicate, please close your issue and add a comment
to the existing issue instead.

If you suspect your issue is a bug, please edit your issue description to
include the BUG REPORT INFORMATION shown below. If you fail to provide this
information within 7 days, we cannot debug your issue and will close it. We
will, however, reopen it if you later provide the information.

---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST
-->

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

With `podman-3.0.0` I'm getting warnings such as:

```
time="2021-02-18T10:12:37Z" level=warning msg="missing \"ROOT\" build argument. Try adding \"--build-arg ROOT=<VALUE>\" to the command line"
time="2021-02-18T10:12:37Z" level=warning msg="missing \"env_name\" build argument. Try adding \"--build-arg env_name=<VALUE>\" to the command line"
time="2021-02-18T10:12:37Z" level=warning msg="missing \"ROOT\" build argument. Try adding \"--build-arg ROOT=<VALUE>\" to the command line"
time="2021-02-18T10:12:37Z" level=warning msg="missing \"SYSROOT\" build argument. Try adding \"--build-arg SYSROOT=<VALUE>\" to the command line"
```

... from multi-stage Dockerfile files containing, e.g.:

```
ARG ROOT="/some/value"
...

FROM image1
ARG ROOT
...

FROM image2
ARG ROOT
```

... which is, by my understanding, the correct means by which multi-stage builds should inherit (Dockerfile) global arguments, in a Don't Repeat Yourself manner.

The warning in this case appears spurious - the values are present and inherited from the global scope.

**Output of `buildah version`:**

```
Version:         1.19.4
Go Version:      go1.15.7
Image Spec:      1.0.1-dev
Runtime Spec:    1.0.2-dev
CNI Spec:        0.4.0
libcni Version:  v0.8.1
image Version:   5.10.1
Git Commit:      175c3e87390ee999b6fd55243bb314af1f7607a0
Built:           Tue Feb  9 14:03:11 2021
OS/Arch:         linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman version` if reporting a `podman build` issue:**

```
Version:      3.0.0
API Version:  3.0.0
Go Version:   go1.15.7
Git Commit:   5b2585f5e91ca148f068cefa647c23f8b1ade622
Built:        Sun Feb 14 20:44:18 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

```

**Output of `cat /etc/containers/storage.conf`:**

```

comments.body: Are you saying that the arguments are working, you just see bad warnings?

Yes - the build completes successfully regardless.

But it feels as if we shouldn't be generating dubious warnings for legitimate configuration, even though it's largely a cosmetic issue.
Yes I agree.  It might be best if these WARNINGS should be INFO.

Should they even be 'INFO'?

'DEBUG' at best?  It is, after all, simply confirming that a stage in a multi-stage build has inherited a global variable... is that sufficiently 'INFO' worthy?

(Now, inheriting an *unset* global variable, that's a different thing!)

(It's a legitimate warning for single-stage builds, of course)

Does this break down to "We should warn when an argument without a value _at the point of reference_ is encountered"?

Yes we should only call this function on the first stage of a multi stage build, sadly I don't know this part of the code that well.

	warnOnUnsetBuildArgs(mainNode, options.Args)
@TomSweeneyRedHat @nalind is there an easy way in the imagebuilder code to check if you are in the fist stage of a multi-stage build?
When I looked into the problem of showing how many stages a build would have before the build started, I could determine the first stage, but not the total if I recall correctly.

We definitely don't want to downgrade those warnings as if there wasn't a global definition made, they'd hold. I think we also warn already on when an ARG is unused at the moment, but could be proved wrong.  I think the one spot that should be concentrated on in the short term is not throwing the error if it's a global and already defined arg.

I don't have a quick fix for that, will need to dive through code.
@TomSweeneyRedHat @srcshelton Could one of you open a PR to not warn if the ARG is set.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@TomSweeneyRedHat @srcshelton Any time to work on this?
On my list, but hasn't bubbled up.
